Tafseer:
The divine law regarding nations:
This describes the divine law that has been explained in various places in the Holy Qur'an—namely, that when the moral corruption of a nation reaches such a level that they deserve Allah Almighty's decisive punishment, then before sending the punishment, Allah Almighty establishes His proof by sending a messenger among them, so that no one has any excuse to remain in deviation. In accordance with this law, before sending the punishment upon the nation of Noah, He sent Noah (peace be upon him) as a messenger to warn his people that Allah's punishment was indeed about to come. If they desired salvation from it, they should abandon their path of deviation and follow the way he was calling to; otherwise, they should remember that there is no escape for anyone from Allah's wrath.

The warning of Noah (peace be upon him):
The explanation of 'a clear warner' (nadhirum-mubeen) was presented in its relevant place. (Footnote 1) Noah (peace be upon him) warned his people in accordance with the command of Allah Almighty, saying: 'Just as a warner who strips himself [to draw his nation's attention to an incoming threat] warns his nation of an invading enemy, similarly I am a clear warner for you, and Allah has sent me to you to warn you of the punishment that is hovering over your heads.'
_____
Footnote 1: Refer to Tadabbur-i-Qur'an, Vol. 3, p. 386; Vol. 4, p. 404.

This is the detail of that warning: worship Allah, avoid violating His limits, and obey me.
'Worship Allah' (an-i'budu-llah) - That is: abandon the worship of your self-carved idols (whose details are coming in this Surah) and worship your true Lord, Allah alone. There is no deity besides Him.
'And fear Him' (wa-ttaquh) - That is: observe the limits and boundaries Allah has prescribed for the guidance of your life, so that you remain secure from His wrath. The actual meaning of 'taqwa' (fear of God/piety)—as we have clarified in various places in this book—is that the servant should fully respect the limits Allah has established for His servants in every aspect of life through His Shariah, remaining constantly fearful of violating them. Those who become bold in violating these limits eventually fall under the grasp of Allah Almighty's punishment.
'And obey me' (wa atee'un) - That is: abandon the obedience of your corrupt leaders and obey me.
Further on in this Surah, these corrupt leaders are mentioned. Noah (peace be upon him) also warned his people: 'Your leaders are leading you toward God's punishment. If you wish to be saved from this punishment, abandon their following and choose the path to which I am calling you.'
The three pillars of Noah's call: The three fundamental pillars of Noah's call are explained in this verse: monotheism, observing the Shariah of Allah, and obeying the Messenger. The call of all messengers has been based on these three pillars. The stability of religion depends on their stability. As long as a nation stands firm upon them, its steps remain firm on the straight path. Once its steps slip, its path becomes crooked, until it drifts so far from the true path that no possibility of return remains. Then, it rejects the advice of even the best advisors and ultimately falls under the grasp of God's punishment.

A linguistic discussion:
That is: if you accept these three matters of mine, Allah Almighty will forgive the sins for which you were declared deserving of punishment, and He will grant you respite to live and prosper in this world for a specified term.
In 'He will forgive you of your sins' (yaghfir lakum-min dhunoobikum), some have regarded the particle 'min' (of) as redundant, while others have taken it in the meaning of 'an' (from). However, both these opinions are contrary to Arabic grammar. No letter in the Qur'an is redundant. If a letter appears redundant on the surface, it is there to fill a gap under a recognized rule of the language. Such letters are specific; every letter cannot be declared redundant without authority. There is no example of 'min' being redundant in the Qur'an or in authoritative speech.
Similarly, taking it in the meaning of 'an' is also completely unauthorized. First, there is no reliable example of it being used in the meaning of 'an', and even if there were, the verb 'ghafara' (to forgive) does not take the preposition 'an'. In supplication, you say: 'O our Lord, forgive us our sins' (Rabbana-ghfir lana dhunoobana), you do not say: 'Oour Lord, forgive us from our sins' (Rabbana-ghfir lana 'an dhunoobina). If you were to say so, to prove it correct you would have to argue that 'ghafara' implies the meaning of 'safaha' (to pardon) or another similar verb that takes 'an'. Without this, using 'an' with 'ghafara' is contrary to Arabic grammar.
In my view, 'min' here is used in its well-known meaning of partitive (tab'eed). The full sentence is as if:
'He will forgive you what has passed of your sins' (ma taqaddama min dhunoobikum).
Here, based on context, the words 'what has passed' (ma taqaddama) have been omitted, as it is well known and intellectually sound that choosing a life of faith after disbelief forgives the sins a person committed during their pre-Islamic life. As for the sins a person commits after choosing a life of faith, there is a specific rule for their forgiveness, as explained in the verse: (Repentance is only [accepted] by Allah for those who do wrong in ignorance and then repent soon after. It is those to whom Allah will turn in forgiveness, and Allah is ever Knowing and Wise) (An-Nisa, 4:17), which was clarified in its relevant place. In the verse under discussion, the particle 'min' is used to show that if you accept this call and enter into faith, your sins of the pre-Islamic period will be forgiven. If 'min' were not here, the verse could have implied that all your past and future sins would be forgiven, which is incorrect because faith after disbelief only destroys past sins, not future ones.
Every opportunity in this world is transient: 'and delay you for a specified term' (wa yu'akhkhirkum ila ajalim-musamma) - That is: if you accept these three matters of mine, Allah Almighty will delay the punishment of which I am warning you, and grant you respite to live and prosper in this world for a specified term.
The restriction 'specified term' manifests the reality that no respite in this world is limitless. This world and everything in it is temporary and transient. Even if a person leads a life of faith and righteous deeds, he is not granted limitless life here; rather, he must inevitably return his soul to the Creator one day. However, he is not destroyed by a divine punishment, but is allowed to benefit from his lifespan. Similarly, if a nation chooses a life of faith, piety, and obedience to the Messenger, Allah Almighty grants them prosperity as long as they remain firm on faith and piety. As soon as they deviate, signs of decline begin to appear, until their moral decline reaches the final point, when their 'specified term' expires and their existence as a nation is erased from the face of the earth. The same is the case with this entire world: its term is also specified. A Day will come when this world of trial will be folded up, and a new world will appear with completely new laws, which is called the Hereafter.
Noah (peace be upon him) drew attention to the reality that every opportunity in this world is limited and transient. Both the righteous and the wicked should keep this in mind. Those who keep it in mind will benefit from this life's respite, while those who forget it will find this world to be a source of misery and loss.
'If you only knew' (law kuntum ta'lamun) - The true secret of this life's success is hidden within this point, but those who understand it are very few; therefore, Noah (peace be upon him) expressed his wish: 'Would that you knew and understood!'
The divine law described in this verse was also referenced in Surah An-Nahl, verse 61; refer to it for details.

The second phase of Noah's call: This begins the second phase of Noah's call, when he presented his case to his Lord—stating that all his day and night efforts in delivering the call were completely ineffective on his people. It should be noted that there is no known example of a call being delivered for as long a duration as Noah (peace be upon him) delivered to his people, as the Qur'an explicitly states:
(And he remained among them a thousand years less fifty years) (Al-Ankabut, 29:14).
In 'I have called my people night and day' (da'awtu qawmi laylaw-wa-nahara), there is obviously no trace of exaggeration. Since a messenger comes as a court of justice for his people, and their life or death depends on accepting or rejecting him, every messenger has spent his days and nights in delivering the call, exhausting every ounce of his energy; but Noah (peace be upon him) is distinguished in that he warned and awakened his people for such a long duration that has no parallel, as noted. Yet, the outcome of this effort, in his own words, was:
(but my call did not increase them except in flight [from the truth]).
This flight and evasion, obviously, was not because there was anything contrary to reason and nature in Noah's call, or because they had any doubt about his sincerity and truthfulness. The rationality of his call was clear to everyone, and everyone acknowledged his truthfulness in their hearts. However, because his call was contrary to their desires, and accepting it would deal a blow to the pride of their leaders, Noah (peace be upon him) met with greater flight the more he pursued them. They felt they had no answer to his words, and changing their lives was impossible for them; so, they assumed safety lay in fleeing, in order to save their guilty conscience from the pain of acknowledging their crime. Although this is a cheap tactic, what other option do those who flee from reality have?
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 5 and 6 was combined in the original text.]

Depiction of the people's flight from the call:
This portrays the flight and pride of the people, with their leaders being the primary focus. It is stated: whenever I called them to repent and seek forgiveness so that You might forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted in their ways, and showed great arrogance.
In 'I called them that You might forgive them' (da'awtuhum li-taghfira lahum), a portion of the discourse is omitted for the sake of eloquence. If unpacked, the full sentence is: 'whenever I called people to repent and seek forgiveness so that they might become worthy of Your forgiveness, they put their fingers in their ears.' But instead of saying it that way, Noah (peace be upon him) replaced the action with the result of the action, to make the people's misery and deprivation entirely clear: 'I called them to make them worthy of Your mercy and forgiveness, but they turned out to be so unfortunate that they refused to even listen to my words.'
'And they covered themselves with their garments' (wa-staghshaw thiyabahum) - This portrays the pride of the leaders. The meaning is: as soon as they heard my words, they covered themselves with their garments in disgust and walked away.
'And they persisted and were arrogant with [great] arrogance' (wa asarru wa-stakbaru-stikbara) - After 'asarru' (they persisted), a verbal noun (masdar) is omitted, as is: 'asarru israra'. Since the verbal noun is mentioned after 'stakbaru', it was omitted in the first place. The meaning is: these people persisted in their shirk and disobedience instead of accepting my call to seek forgiveness.
The meaning of 'istikbar': 'Istikbar' means knowingly opposing the truth and acting with rebelliousness against it. Whether the truth is small or large, it is approved and demanded by Allah; therefore, the servant's duty is to submit to it, even if it is highly difficult for his desires. If someone displays pride against the truth, he follows the way of Satan and will share his fate.
This mention of pride serves as the cause behind the actions described earlier—showing that putting fingers in their ears, covering themselves with their garments, and persisting in shirk occurred because they possessed intense pride.

The third phase of the call:
In highly eloquent words, Noah (peace be upon him) pointed to the third phase of his call: when I saw that they had put their fingers in their ears and covered themselves with their garments, I made the tone of my call louder, sharper, and more energetic:
'Sing the song louder when you see the caravan is heavy!'
The way of the noble prophets (peace be upon them) has always been that the more their nation's disgust toward the call increased, the more their energy for the call multiplied, their tone became louder and more heart-shaking, and their compassion increased. This is the nature of the truth and the people of truth. The intensity of opposition highlights the power of the truth and inspires the resolve of the people of truth rather than suppressing it:
'When my nature is obstructed, it flows even faster.'

'Then indeed, I announced to them and confided to them secretly' (thumma inni a'lantu lahum wa asrartu lahum israra) - That is: where there was a need to proclaim the message openly, I did so without hesitation, so that my voice would reach even the deaf; and where I saw an opportunity to approach them individually and explain the matter privately, I tried that method as well, so that those who possessed any spark of life could reflect on their destiny before the decision arrived. In short, I tried every method—both gentle and firm, public and private—so that no shortcoming remained in my duty of communication.
In my view, after 'a'lantu lahum' (I announced to them), a verbal noun is omitted, just as it was omitted after 'asarru' above.

Compassion in the call:
This explains the call Noah (peace be upon him) delivered to his people with great compassion and love. It is stated: I advised them: 'O my people, ask forgiveness of your Lord for your sins. No matter how many your sins are, do not despair of His mercy. Indeed, He is ever Forgiving.'
'Indeed, He is ever Forgiving' (innahu kana ghaffara) also implies that to receive His forgiveness, your turning to Him is sufficient; there is no need for the intercession of your self-carved goddesses and gods. He is the Greatest Forgiver. Those who seek forgiveness with a sincere heart are protected by Him within the veil of His mercy without needing any intercessor.
There is no forgiver greater than God, such that His intercession should be sought: It should be noted that the polytheists of every age have suffered from the delusion that since God's presence is highly elevated, it is impossible for an individual to have his supplication accepted by Him unless there are some intercessors.
(We only worship them that they may bring us nearer to Allah in position) (Az-Zumar, 39:3)
represents their belief. The Qur'an has refuted this delusion in various ways. Here, 'Indeed, He is ever Forgiving' also refutes it—showing that since He is the Greatest Forgiver, who is there greater than Him who can serve as an intercessor with Him.

The blessing of seeking forgiveness: 'He will send [rain from] the heaven upon you in abundance' (yursili-s-sama'u 'alaykum-midrara) - As clarified in its relevant place, 'sama' (heaven) is also used for rain clouds. 'Midrar' means pouring or raining abundantly, and it is used identically for both masculine and feminine. The meaning is: your seeking forgiveness will arouse the mercy of your Lord, and He will bless you with abundance of rain, wealth, and children.
Refutation of some polytheistic delusions: The polytheists also suffered from the delusion that rain is sent by their idols and children are granted by their blessings; therefore, they were terrified of saying or hearing a single word against them. The Qur'an indicates that they opposed the messengers out of fear that they criticized the idols, who would grow angry and deprive the people of their blessings. Indeed, during the time of the messengers, if any trial befell them, they attributed it to the ill omen of the Messenger and his companions, claiming they had displeased the deities. With this statement, Noah (peace be upon him) dealt a blow to their delusion: 'The treasures of rain, wealth, and children are not possessed by your deities, such that if you abandon them, they will deprive you of these blessings. The Owner of all these things is Allah alone, and His mercy is attained through repentance and seeking forgiveness. Do this, and see how the clouds of His mercy gather and pour upon you!' This theme appeared in Surah Hud, verse 52; refer to it for details.
Here, a point of religious wisdom from the words of Caliph Umar is worth preserving. Narrations state that he once performed only 'istighfar' (seeking forgiveness) during the prayer for rain, without mentioning rain in his supplication. People asked: 'O Commander of the Faithful, you did not mention rain in your supplication!' He informed them, in the light of these verses, that the key to God's mercy is seeking forgiveness, and this is what we have performed. The meaning is: this seeking of forgiveness will attract His mercy, and our Lord knows our needs and poverty better than we do.

This continues the preceding theme: wealth, children, gardens, and canals are all granted by Allah alone. If you please your Lord through repentance and seeking forgiveness, you will receive all these things. None of these things are granted by your deities, such that they would withhold them if displeased.
Expressing amazement at the foolishness of the stubborn: In 'And make for you gardens and make for you rivers' (wa yaj'al-lakum jannatiw-wa yaj'al-lakum anhara), the repetition of the verb highlights the value and desirability of these blessings, which is apparent to those who possess a taste for Arabic grammar.

This expresses amazement at the stubbornness of the people: 'What is the matter with you that I am calling you to seek forgiveness with such dedication, yet you have put oil in your ears and are ignoring my words! Do you assume that you will continue to enjoy your luxuries, and the majesty of the Lord Who gave you all of this will never manifest despite your rebelliousness?' The meaning is: if you assume so, it means you have no concept of His greatness and majesty in your minds. You regard Him as completely indifferent and helpless, assuming you can cause whatever corruption you wish in His world but His honor will never be aroused. This same theme is expressed in the verse:
(O man, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous) (Al-Infitar, 82:6).
That is: when a person sees blessings all around him and finds himself doing whatever he wishes without being seized, he gradually grows bold and untroubled by God's grasp. Yet, if he reflected, he would easily understand that God displayed His generosity so that the servant might become grateful and expect that he will be questioned about these blessings one day, and on that Day, no one will save the ungrateful from His grasp.
'Tarjun' here means to expect or look forward to, and 'waqar' means greatness, status, and majesty. Allah possesses the attributes of majesty alongside those of beauty, which manifest for those who disobey His messengers and act arrogantly. To remain firm on the straight path, it is necessary to remember both of these categories of attributes. If imbalance occurs, a person's concept of divine attributes becomes distorted, which ruins the balance of his entire life. Since this addresses the rebels, reference is made specifically to the attribute of majesty.

Proof of the manifestation of divine majesty:
This is the proof showing why the Day of the manifestation of His majesty is not impossible. It is stated: if you reflect on the stages of your own creation, you can easily understand that the God Who displayed His power within your own existence, what difficulty can it be for Him to resurrect you after you have turned to dust, so that you witness His majesty as He recompenses the rebels and wrongdoers! This proof has been explained in detail in other places in the Qur'an to prove the Resurrection. In Surah Al-Hajj, He states:
(O people, if you should be in doubt about the Resurrection, then [consider] that We created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then from a clinging clot, and then from a lump of flesh, formed and unformed—that We may show you. And We settle in the wombs whom We will for a specified term, then We bring you out as a child, then [We develop you] that you may reach your [time of] maturity. And among you is he who is taken in [early] death, and among you is he who is returned to the most decrepit [old] age so that he knows, after [having had] knowledge, nothing. And you see the earth barren, but when We send down upon it rain, it quivers and swells and grows [something] of every beautiful kind. That is because Allah is the Truth and because He gives life to the dead and because He is over all things competent.) (Al-Hajj, 22:5-6).
This same theme appeared in Surah Al-Mu'minun, verses 14-16; refer to it for details.

Some parenthetical verses:
These six verses can be regarded as part of the speech of Noah (peace be upon him), but my mind inclines to the view that they are parenthetical verses revealed by Allah Almighty to complete Noah's speech. Examples of such parenthetical insertions have appeared previously. The indication for this is that Noah's speech begins again in verse 21 with: (Noah said, 'My Lord'). If verses 15-20 were also part of his speech, there would be no need for repeating 'Noah said, My Lord'. This repetition is intended to show that the preceding verses were parenthetical, and to connect the subsequent part with Noah's supplication, it is clarified that this is Noah's statement.
The most prominent sign of divine power: 'Do you not consider how Allah has created seven heavens in layers' (alam taraw kayfa khalaqa-llahu sab'a samawatin tibaqan) - This draws attention to the most prominent sign of His creation: can the God Who was powerful over creating seven heavens, layer upon layer, find it difficult to resurrect you? This is the same proof as:
(Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it) (An-Naziat, 79:27),
and which also appeared with a different style in the previous Surah, verses 40-41.
'Tibaqan' means layer upon layer. This word does not require that the heavens are physical layers like sheets of cloth, but rather that there are seven distinct worlds, one above the other, with seven distinct heavens. Such matters are explained in the Qur'an to establish a basic concept of Allah's limitless power, and general belief in them is sufficient. The actual reality will manifest on the Day the veil is lifted, and no one besides Allah Almighty can lift this veil. Currently, the reach of our science is very limited, and whatever reach it has attained has only increased human amazement rather than fully explaining the reality. The actual reality will open in the Hereafter.

Reference to two great signs within the heavens:
'And made the moon therein a light and made the sun a lamp' (wa ja'ala-l-qamara feehinna nooraw-wa ja'ala-sh-shamsa siraja) - After drawing attention to the heavens, attention is drawn to two great signs within them: He is the One Who made the moon a light and the sun a lamp within these heavens to illuminate them. This is a sign of His great power, as well as His limitless wisdom and universal providence—showing that if He had not established the moon and sun as lamps, this world would have been in pitch darkness. For those to whom the Resurrection seems impossible despite these bright signs, nothing can open their eyes. It should noted that the signs of His mercy and providence in this universe make the Day of Justice necessary; this has been discussed in detail in its relevant place.

Reference to the signs on earth:
'And Allah has caused you to grow from the earth a [progressive] growth. Then He will return you into it and extract you [another] extraction' (wa-llahu ambatakum-minal-ardi nabata, thumma yu'eedukum feeha wa yukhrijukum ikhraja) - After drawing attention to the heaven and its signs, this draws attention to the signs on earth, starting first with the most noble creation of the earth—man himself. It is stated: Allah caused you to grow from the earth, then He will return you into it after death, and then He will extract you from it one day.
The claim is also the proof: This is the pinnacle of the Qur'an's eloquence—showing that the claim in this verse is itself the highly clear proof of the claim. If unpacked, the full meaning is: just as vegetation grows from the earth, similarly Allah Almighty caused you to grow from this very earth, and just as vegetation decays and merges into the earth, similarly you too will die and turn to dust in the earth. Then, just as you see that Allah Almighty revives the decayed vegetation whenever He wills, similarly He will resurrect you without any difficulty whenever He wills.
'Nabatan' and 'ikhrajan' are used for emphasis (ta'keed), and the aspects of emphasis are diverse, making their translation into another language difficult. For example, 'ambatakum nabatan' can mean: He caused you to grow with great power and wisdom, or with great ease, or with great care. I have preferred the aspect of care, power, and wisdom in the translation because Qur'anic precedents support this. This was detailed under: 'And He has created you in stages'. In 'yukhrijukum ikhrajan', I have kept in view the aspect of ease—meaning Allah Almighty will resurrect you with great ease whenever He wills. In preferring this aspect, I have also kept in view the Qur'anic precedents; although other aspects are possible, no translation seemed to encompass all aspects.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 17 and 18 was combined in the original text.]

The earth itself is a great sign:
'And Allah has made for you the earth a carpet, that you may go along therein in spacious paths' (wa-llahu ja'ala lakumu-l-arda bisata, li-tasluku minha subulan fijaja) - This draws attention to the sign of the earth itself, which manifests Allah Almighty's great power, wisdom, mercy, and favor. It is stated: We made the earth a carpet for you, fixing mountains in it to keep it balanced and stable, and establishing passes and paths within the mountains so that you are not locked behind mountain walls but can pass through the valleys to travel from place to place.
It should noted that for the earth to serve as a carpet, it must be stable; therefore, the Qur'an states that Allah fixed mountains in it to preserve its balance: (lest it should sway with you) (An-Nahl, 16:15) and (Have We not made the earth a resting place and the mountains as pegs) (An-Naba, 78:6-7). This arrangement was also accompanied by establishing passes and paths in the mountains so that the ways of travel remain open for humans. The verse uses the word 'fijaj', which is the plural of 'fajj'. This word is used specifically for mountain passes and paths, as explained under 'a distant mountain pass' in Surah Al-Hajj.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19 and 20 was combined in the original text.]

Continuing Noah's supplication after the parenthetical verses:
The parenthetical verses ended here, and the discourse connects back to Noah's supplication. Thus, 'Noah said, My Lord' is repeated to distinguish it. 
Noah (peace be upon him) mentioned the reaction shown by his people during the third phase of his call—which was the final phase of establishing the proof. He stated: 'O my Lord! I have tried every effort, but these hard-hearted individuals did not listen to my words; instead, they followed their leaders, whose abundance of wealth and children only increased them in loss.' That is: wealth and children, instead of inspiring gratitude, increased their pride, because of which they persisted in their ways and refused to listen to my words, as stated in Surah Al-Qalam:
(Because he was a possessor of wealth and children, when Our verses are recited to him, he says, 'Stories of the ancients')
(meaning: because they possess wealth and children, when they are warned of the end of those who denied the messengers and are narrated the accounts of past nations, they say with pride: 'These are stories of the ancients; we are not going to be intimidated by them').

'Kubbar' is the intensive form of 'kabeer'. That is: to defeat my call, turn their common people away from me, and incite them against me, they planned a mighty plot. There is no detail of these plots here, but they are not difficult to understand. The plots hatched by the arrogant of every age to turn people away from the truth can help us understand the plots of the nation of Noah.

This explains the details of the stubbornness of the leaders mentioned in verse 7: they incited their common people against my call to monotheism, saying: 'Do not abandon your deities for this person.'
The hardiness of the idols of Noah's nation:
'And they said, "Never leave your gods and never leave Wadd or Suwa' or Yaghuth and Ya'uq and Nasr"' - These are the names of their major idols. Their influence was firmly established in the hearts of the common people; therefore, the leaders named them specifically to incite the common people, saying: 'Stand firm upon your ancestral deities. If you show weakness, your ancestral religion will be endangered.'
These names are clearly Arabic names. The dwelling of the nation of Noah was in northern Hijaz; therefore, it is possible their language was Arabic. The hardiness of these idols is remarkable: the flood erased every trace of the nation of Noah, but the influence of these idols remained, and history shows that their worship was later revived among various Arab tribes. Thus, Wadd was the idol of the tribe of Banu Kalb (a branch of Quda'ah). Suwa' was worshipped by Hudhayl. Yaghuth was the idol of some branches of Tayy. Ya'uq belonged to a branch of Hamdan. Nasr was worshipped by a branch of Himyar. The order in which they are mentioned here suggests their ranks in the nation of Noah—showing that Wadd and Suwa' held the highest ranks, while Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr were below them.

The supplication of Noah against them:
This expresses the deep sorrow Noah (peace be upon him) felt over the attitude of the leaders, and from his tongue emerged this supplication: 'O my Lord, increase them only in deviation so that they hasten toward their punishment, and the earth is cleansed of their impurity as soon as possible.'
Allah Almighty did not create humans to remain as a burden on this earth, but to strive for success. A human has the right to remain on earth only as long as some spark of goodness remains in him. Once this spark is completely lost, his existence becomes a curse for the earth. A messenger serves as the final means of establishing the proof; therefore, after he sifts the people, the grain is separated from the chaff, and he burns the chaff. Noah (peace be upon him), reaching the third phase of his call, saw that whatever potential for goodness they possessed had emerged, and what remained was useless; therefore, their destruction was for the best.
At a similar stage, Prophet Moses (peace be upon him) also prayed against the nation of Pharaoh, as recorded in Surah Yunus:
(Our Lord, obliterate their wealth and harden their hearts so that they will not believe until they see the painful punishment.) (Yunus, 10:88).

The immediate acceptance of the supplication:
This verse is not part of Noah's supplication, but is a parenthetical insertion by Allah Almighty after his first statement. It is intended to show that Noah's supplication was offered at the correct time for a correct purpose; therefore, as soon as the first statement emerged from his tongue, the entire supplication was accepted. If this glad tiding were placed at the end of the supplication, the aspect of immediate acceptance would not be prominent; therefore, it was placed after his first statement. Examples of such insertions have appeared previously.
'Because of their sins they were drowned and put into the Fire' (mim-ma khati'atihim ughriqu fa-udkhilu nara) - It is stated: these people, because of their crimes, were drowned in water and cast into the Fire—showing that they faced both punishments. In this world, they drowned in water, and in the Hereafter, they will be cast into the Fire.
'And they found not for themselves besides Allah any helpers' (fa-lam yajidu lahum-min dooni-llahi ansara) - That is: when they were warned of the punishment, they behaved arrogantly based on their power, population, and deities, but when Allah's punishment arrived, no one arose to help them against Him.

Completion of the supplication:
After the parenthetical insertion, Noah's supplication resumes, marked by 'Noah said, My Lord'. If the parenthetical insertion were not there, there would be no need for repeating these words; they were repeated to avoid confusion.
He prayed: 'O my Lord! Do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers a companion.' In Arabic, if you say 'ma fi-d-dari dayyar', it means there is no one in the house. The reason for praying for their complete destruction is what we pointed out above: a messenger sifts the entire nation, serving as the final means of establishing the proof, after which only the punishment remains. He prayed: 'If You leave them, they will only mislead Your servants and will not beget except wicked disbelievers.' It should noted that although every child is born upon the natural disposition (fitrah), the upbringing of parents and the environment play the primary role in their formation. If the environment is good, the child will grow upon faith; and if the environment is corrupt, the child will follow its color, as stated in the Hadith: (His parents make him a Jew or a Christian). Noah (peace be upon him) had thoroughly sifted his society and saw that no potential for goodness remained in it; therefore, he said: 'They will only beget wicked disbelievers. No believer will emerge from their wombs.'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 26 and 27 was combined in the original text.]

Finally, Noah (peace be upon him) prayed for his own forgiveness, for his parents, for whoever entered his house as a believer, and for all believing men and women, concluding his supplication with a prayer for the destruction of those who invited it by persisting in shirk and disbelief.
The great right of parents: 'and my parents' (wa li-walidayya) - Noah's prayer for his parents manifests their great right, which is emphasized repeatedly in the Qur'an. Some believe his parents were believers, but there is no explicit indication of this in the Qur'an. It is possible they passed away before his call or before the proof was established; in both cases, praying for their forgiveness was a requirement of his duty toward them, especially when there was no prohibition from Allah Almighty.
'and whoever enters my house as a believer' (wa liman dakhala baytiya mu'mina) shows that in the final phase, Noah (peace be upon him) had announced: those who seek refuge from the punishment should enter his house as believers before the punishment appears.
